narrative:
As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 41, which exceeds the dataset average of 36.70, you've shown real academic promise in several areas. Your family comes from a lower-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students at 92.7%. However, the university's admission model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 68.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass on their first try.

The model's decision hinged most strongly on your performance rankings within your school: specifically, your Year 3 grades placed you in the bottom decile (1st out of 10), far below the average 5th or 6th decile, and this was the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure. Your Year 1 grades, also in a low decile (2nd out of 10) compared to the typical mid-range, reinforced that concern as the third-most influential element. On a brighter note, your standout LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other positive factor.

In contrast to typical students who pass the bar, where consistent mid-to-high school deciles often align with bar success, your early and later undergraduate rankings signal potential challenges in sustaining performance under law school pressure. While your GPA and LSAT shine brighter than average, they weren't enough to offset those lower in-school rankings in the model's eyes.

To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking targeted tutoring to boost your school deciles, as improving those could dramatically shift future predictions. You might also explore programs with bar prep support tailored for students from similar backgrounds, or apply to schools with more holistic reviews beyond this model's metrics. With your strong LSAT and GPA as a foundation, targeted improvements here could open doors soon.
